content.full_text:
Memorandum

To: File

Date: Dec. 15, 1978

From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Ted Prim

Subject: Principal place of residence

At the Commission meeting of December 5, 1978, in the course of approving the conflict of interest code for the Mid-Peninsula Regional Openspace District, the Commission discussed the provision of the Act which exempts an official's principal place of residence from disclosure.

The Commission concluded that a district such as MROSD could include a special provision in its code specifying that the principal place of residence only referred to the residence and three acres of land surrounding the residence. This was because the district had special powers to affect the value of real property including personal residences.

As a general rule, the Commission indicated that the property around a residence was exempted from disclosure as part of the personal place of residence unless it were sufficiently large to be considered an interest in real property separate from the residence. In determining whether the property is an interest in real property separate from the residence, consideration should be given to the following factors: whether the property is subdividable; minimum lot size under zoning ordinances; the size of nearby lots; and, whether the property appears to be held for investment purposes because it can later be sold for speculation or income producing purposes.

Property which is income producing must be disclosed although the value of the personal residence may be subtracted for the purposes of computing the value of the income producing property.

TP:kp

sections.question_synthetic: What constitutes a 'principal place of residence' exempt from disclosure under the Political Reform Act, particularly regarding land surrounding a residence and how should districts apply this exemption?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that districts may specify principal place of residence exemptions to include the residence and up to three acres of surrounding land, and provided factors to determine when surrounding property constitutes a separate real property interest requiring disclosure (subdividability, zoning minimums, comparable lot sizes, and investment intent), while noting that income-producing property must always be disclosed.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal FPPC memorandum documenting Commission meeting conclusions rather than a formal response to an external request; no explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections but clear guidance provided on principal residence exemption standards; formatted as internal memo to file.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What constitutes a 'principal place of residence' exempt from disclosure under the Political Reform Act, particularly regarding land surrounding a residence and how should districts apply this exemption?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concluded that districts may specify principal place of residence exemptions to include the residence and up to three acres of surrounding land, and provided factors to determine when surrounding property constitutes a separate real property interest requiring disclosure (subdividability, zoning minimums, comparable lot sizes, and investment intent), while noting that income-producing property must always be disclosed.

The Commission concluded that a district such as MROSD could include a special provision in its code specifying that the principal place of residence only referred to the residence and three acres of land surrounding the residence. This was because the district had special powers to affect the value of real property including personal residences. As a general rule, the Commission indicated that the property around a residence was exempted from disclosure as part of the personal place of residence unless it were sufficiently large to be considered an interest in real property separate from the residence. In determining whether the property is an interest in real property separate from the residence, consideration should be given to the following factors: whether the property is subdividable; minimum lot size under zoning ordinances; the size of nearby lots; and, whether the property appears to be held for investment purposes because it can later be sold for speculation or income producing purposes. Property which is income producing must be disclosed although the value of the personal residence may be subtracted for the purposes of computing the value of the income producing property. TP:kp
embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum addressing the exemption for principal place of residence from conflict of interest code disclosure requirements, establishing that surrounding property up to three acres may be included and providing factors to distinguish exempt residential property from separately held real property interests.
